name: Malaysia
tagline: From the Malacca Sultanate and the spice straits to a multiethnic federation of peninsula and Borneo.
summary: Malaysia's history is bound to the Strait of Malacca, the maritime artery between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea, where early Malay polities and the Buddhist realm of Srivijaya gave way around 1400 to the Malacca Sultanate, the great Islamic entrepot of the Malay world. Successive European powers seized that wealth, the Portuguese in 1511, the Dutch in 1641 and the British across the nineteenth century, who knit the Straits Settlements, the Malay states and the Borneo territories of Sabah and Sarawak into British Malaya. After the Japanese occupation and the long Malayan Emergency, the Federation of Malaya won independence on 31 August 1957 and Malaysia itself was formed on 16 September 1963; Singapore separated in 1965. Today Malaysia is a multiethnic constitutional monarchy and federation shaped by the New Economic Policy, the Mahathir era and a turbulent modern democracy.

founding.label: Formation of Malaysia
founding.detail: Malaysia was created on 16 September 1963 when the independent Federation of Malaya joined with Singapore and the former British Borneo territories of North Borneo (renamed Sabah) and Sarawak under the Malaysia Agreement signed in London on 9 July 1963. The new federation grew directly out of the Federation of Malaya, which had achieved independence (Merdeka) on 31 August 1957 under Tunku Abdul Rahman. Singapore left the federation by mutual agreement in 1965, leaving the present union of eleven peninsular states and the two Borneo states of Sabah and Sarawak.

quickFacts.label: Strategic geography
quickFacts.value: Astride the Strait of Malacca, a key world trade route

quickFacts.label: UNESCO World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Melaka and George Town, Historic Cities of the Straits of Malacca (2008)

eras.title: Early Malay Polities and the Srivijaya Maritime Realm
eras.standfirst: Long before sultans or colonisers, the wealth of the Malay world flowed through the Strait of Malacca, where the Buddhist thalassocracy of Srivijaya grew rich on the monsoon trade between India and China.
body: The Malay Peninsula and the coasts of Borneo lie astride one of the world's great maritime crossroads, the Strait of Malacca, through which the monsoon trade between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea has passed for two thousand years. From early in the first millennium CE, Indian religious and political ideas blended with local Austronesian traditions to produce a succession of trading polities along these shores, whose rulers drew prestige and wealth from controlling the passage of ships and the exchange of forest products, gold, spices and Chinese goods.

From around the seventh century the Buddhist kingdom of Srivijaya, based at Palembang in southern Sumatra, rose to dominate the sea lanes through the Straits of Malacca and Sunda. By charging tolls on the trade passing through the strait and cultivating ties with both China and India, Srivijaya became the pre-eminent commercial power of the region and a celebrated centre of Mahayana Buddhist learning, drawing pilgrims who broke their journeys there on the way to the great monasteries of India.

Srivijaya's influence reached across the Malay Peninsula and the western archipelago, but from the eleventh century its power waned under pressure from rival states and a southern Indian naval raid, and it was gradually eclipsed by Javanese kingdoms. As the old order dissolved through the fourteenth century, a Sumatran prince fleeing the collapse of these realms would settle at a small fishing village on the peninsula's west coast, and from that refuge would grow the greatest Malay state of all, the Sultanate of Malacca.

events.title: Rise of the Srivijaya thalassocracy
events.summary: Based at Palembang in Sumatra, the Buddhist kingdom of Srivijaya comes to control the Strait of Malacca and the maritime trade between India and China, becoming the dominant commercial power of the Malay world.

events.title: Chola naval raid weakens Srivijaya
events.summary: A naval expedition from the south Indian Chola dynasty strikes Srivijaya's ports, contributing to the long decline of its dominance over the strait.

pullquote.text: Srivijaya's power was based on its control of international sea trade through the Strait of Malacca.
pullquote.attribution: On the maritime kingdom of Srivijaya, c. 7th–11th century

eras.title: The Malacca Sultanate, Islam and the Spice Trade
eras.standfirst: Founded around 1400 by a refugee prince, Malacca became the greatest entrepot of the Malay world, a Muslim sultanate where the spice trade and the coming of Islam met at the narrowest point of the strait.
body: About the year 1400 Paramesvara, a prince who had fled Palembang and then Tumasik (modern Singapore) before the advance of the Javanese kingdom of Majapahit, found refuge at a fishing village on the Malay coast and there founded the city of Malacca. Its position at the chokepoint of the strait, sheltered and well-watered, made it an ideal harbour, and within a few decades it had grown into the busiest and most important port in Southeast Asia, where gold, spices, silk, tin and Chinese goods changed hands.

Malacca's rise was bound up with the spread of Islam, carried along the trade routes by Muslim merchants from India and Arabia. Paramesvara's marriage to a princess of Pasai and the adoption of the title Sultan drew his court and subjects toward the new faith, and Malacca became a springboard from which Islam radiated across the Malay world. Diplomatic and tributary ties with Ming China, marked by the visits of the admiral Zheng He, helped secure the young sultanate's wealth and standing.

At its height Malacca presided over a cosmopolitan trading world, its harbour crowded with ships from China, India, Arabia and the archipelago, its laws and Malay court culture admired and imitated across the region. That very wealth, however, drew the attention of Europeans seeking to seize the spice trade at its source. In 1511 the Portuguese arrived, and the independent sultanate's century-long golden age came to an abrupt and violent end.

events.title: Founding of Malacca by Paramesvara
events.summary: The Sumatran prince Paramesvara, having fled Palembang and Tumasik before Majapahit's forces, settles at Malacca and founds a city that becomes the leading port of the Malay world.

events.title: Malacca, Islam and ties with Ming China
events.summary: Through marriage to a Muslim princess and the adoption of the title Sultan, Malacca's court embraces Islam, while tributary relations with Ming China, marked by Zheng He's visits, secure its trade.

events.title: Malacca, the great entrepot of the strait
events.summary: Malacca grows into the most important trade centre in the region, where gold, spices, silk and tea pass between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea, and Malay court culture flourishes.

figures.name: Paramesvara
figures.role: Founder and first ruler of Malacca
figures.blurb: A prince of Palembang who fled the collapse of the old Sumatran order, by way of Tumasik, and founded Malacca around 1400. His court's turn to Islam helped make Malacca both the leading port of the Malay world and a centre from which the faith spread.

pullquote.text: Malacca became the most important port in Southeast Asia during the 15th and early 16th centuries.

eras.title: European Powers and the Making of British Malaya
eras.standfirst: Portuguese, Dutch and finally British control reshaped the strait. From Penang and Singapore the British built the Straits Settlements, drew the Malay states under protection, and absorbed the Borneo lands of Sabah and Sarawak.
body: European intrusion began in 1511, when the Portuguese admiral Afonso de Albuquerque besieged and stormed Malacca, ending the independent sultanate and seizing the entrepot to gain direct control of the spice trade. The Portuguese held Malacca, often under attack from rival Malay states, until 1641, when the Dutch East India Company captured it in alliance with the Sultanate of Johor. For the Dutch, however, Malacca was secondary to their headquarters at Batavia, and the peninsula's old commercial primacy gradually faded.

British power arrived in 1786, when the East India Company acquired the island of Penang, followed by a settlement at Singapore in 1819 and the formal transfer of Malacca from the Dutch. In 1826 these three ports were combined as the Straits Settlements, the spearhead of British expansion. Across the later nineteenth century Britain extended formal and informal control over the Malay sultanates through a system of British Residents, drawing them into the Federated and Unfederated Malay States, while tin mining and, from the early twentieth century, rubber plantations transformed the economy and drew large numbers of Chinese and Indian immigrants.

Across the South China Sea, the northern coasts of Borneo followed a separate path into the British orbit. In 1841 the English adventurer James Brooke was made raja of Sarawak by the Sultan of Brunei, founding a dynasty of 'White Rajas', while North Borneo (later Sabah) came under a chartered company and then British protection. By the early twentieth century the peninsula and these Borneo territories were bound together loosely under British rule, the multiethnic colonial society from which modern Malaysia would emerge.

events.title: Portuguese capture of Malacca
events.summary: Afonso de Albuquerque storms Malacca after a siege, ending the independent sultanate and beginning sustained European control of the strait and its trade.

events.title: Dutch conquest of Malacca
events.summary: The Dutch East India Company, allied with the Sultanate of Johor, takes Malacca from the Portuguese, holding it as a lesser outpost while focusing power on Batavia.

events.title: Penang, Singapore and the Straits Settlements
events.summary: Britain founds Penang in 1786 and Singapore in 1819 and, with Malacca, combines them in 1826 as the Straits Settlements, the base for expanding British control over the peninsula.

events.title: James Brooke becomes Raja of Sarawak
events.summary: The Sultan of Brunei appoints the Englishman James Brooke raja of Sarawak, founding the 'White Raja' dynasty and beginning the absorption of the Borneo territories into the British sphere.

figures.name: James Brooke
figures.blurb: English adventurer who helped suppress a revolt against the Sultan of Brunei and was rewarded in 1841 with the rule of Sarawak, founding a dynasty that governed the Borneo state until the Second World War and drew it into the territory that became East Malaysia.

eras.title: Occupation, the Emergency and Merdeka
eras.standfirst: The Japanese occupation shattered British prestige; the failed Malayan Union spurred Malay nationalism; and through the long communist Emergency the Federation of Malaya marched to independence on 31 August 1957.
body: In December 1941 Japanese forces under General Tomoyuki Yamashita invaded Malaya, driving down the peninsula with startling speed and forcing the surrender of Singapore on 15 February 1942 in the greatest defeat in British military history. The occupation that followed lasted three and a half years, bringing hardship, repression and a deepening of communal tensions, as Malays and the largely Chinese population experienced Japanese rule very differently. When Japan surrendered in 1945, British prestige had been broken and the old colonial order could not simply be restored.

Postwar Britain proposed a centralised Malayan Union in 1946 that would have weakened the Malay rulers and granted broad citizenship to non-Malays. The fierce Malay reaction gave birth in March 1946 to the United Malays National Organisation (UMNO), the country's first major political party, and forced the scheme's replacement by the Federation of Malaya in 1948. That same year the Malayan Communist Party launched a guerrilla insurgency, and the government declared a state of emergency, beginning the long counterinsurgency known as the Malayan Emergency.

The Emergency, fought through the 1950s with measures such as the resettlement of rural Chinese under the Briggs Plan and the energetic command of Sir Gerald Templer, gradually contained the insurgents. Meanwhile Tunku Abdul Rahman forged the Alliance of UMNO with the Malayan Chinese Association and Malayan Indian Congress, won the 1955 election, and negotiated in London for self-rule. At midnight on 30 August 1957 the Union Jack was lowered in Kuala Lumpur, and on 31 August Tunku Abdul Rahman proclaimed the independence of the Federation of Malaya, shouting 'Merdeka!' seven times before a vast crowd.

events.title: Japanese occupation and the fall of Singapore
events.summary: Japanese forces overrun Malaya and capture Singapore on 15 February 1942, beginning a three-and-a-half-year occupation that shatters British prestige and deepens communal divisions.

events.title: Malayan Union opposition and the founding of UMNO
events.summary: Malay opposition to the centralising Malayan Union proposal gives rise to the United Malays National Organisation, the country's first major political party, and the union is soon replaced by the Federation of Malaya.

events.title: The Malayan Emergency
events.summary: After the Malayan Communist Party launches a guerrilla insurgency, the government declares a state of emergency on 18 June 1948, beginning a counterinsurgency that lasts until 1960.

events.title: Merdeka: independence of Malaya
events.summary: The Federation of Malaya achieves independence under Tunku Abdul Rahman, who proclaims a sovereign, democratic state at Merdeka Stadium in Kuala Lumpur.

figures.name: Tunku Abdul Rahman
figures.role: First Prime Minister of Malaya and Malaysia
figures.blurb: Leader of UMNO and the multiethnic Alliance who negotiated independence in London and proclaimed Merdeka in 1957. Remembered as the 'Father of Independence', he became the first prime minister of Malaya and then of Malaysia, presiding over the federation's formation in 1963.

pullquote.attribution: Tunku Abdul Rahman proclaiming Malayan independence, 31 August 1957

eras.title: The Formation of Malaysia and Singapore's Separation
eras.standfirst: On 16 September 1963 Malaya, Sabah, Sarawak and Singapore joined to form Malaysia, surviving Indonesian Konfrontasi; but communal strains soon forced Singapore out in 1965 and erupted in the riots of 1969.
body: In May 1961 Tunku Abdul Rahman proposed merging the Federation of Malaya with Singapore and the British Borneo territories into a wider federation. After commissions of inquiry and negotiations, the Malaysia Agreement was signed in London on 9 July 1963, and on 16 September 1963 the Federation of Malaysia came into being, uniting the eleven states of Malaya with Singapore and the Borneo states of North Borneo, renamed Sabah, and Sarawak. Brunei chose not to join. This was the founding moment of the modern state.

The new federation was contested from the outset. Indonesia's President Sukarno opposed it as a neocolonial creation and launched the Konfrontasi, an undeclared campaign of armed confrontation along the Borneo border and against the peninsula that lasted until 1966. Within Malaysia, friction quickly grew between the federal government in Kuala Lumpur and the predominantly Chinese state of Singapore, whose leaders championed a 'Malaysian Malaysia' at odds with the special position of the Malays.

The strains proved irreconcilable. On 9 August 1965, after secret negotiations and what Tunku Abdul Rahman called an agonised decision, Singapore was separated from Malaysia by mutual agreement and became an independent state. Four years later, on 13 May 1969, communal rioting between Malays and Chinese erupted in Kuala Lumpur after a tense general election, leaving many dead and prompting a suspension of parliament. The shock of '13 May' reshaped the country's politics and set the stage for a sweeping programme of affirmative action.

events.title: Formation of the Federation of Malaysia
events.summary: Malaya joins with Singapore, Sabah (North Borneo) and Sarawak under the Malaysia Agreement to form Malaysia, the founding act of the modern federation.

events.title: Indonesian Confrontation (Konfrontasi)
events.summary: Indonesia's President Sukarno opposes the new federation and wages an undeclared campaign of armed confrontation against Malaysia until 1966, drawing in Britain, Australia and the United States.

events.title: Singapore's separation from Malaysia
events.summary: Amid deepening communal and political friction, Singapore is separated from Malaysia by mutual agreement and becomes an independent state.

events.title: The 13 May communal riots
events.summary: Racial rioting between Malays and Chinese in Kuala Lumpur follows a divisive general election, leaving many dead and leading to a suspension of parliament that transforms Malaysian politics.

pullquote.text: On 16 September 1963 the Federation of Malaysia was formed, with North Borneo, renamed Sabah, and Sarawak constituting East Malaysia.

eras.title: The New Economic Policy, Mahathir and Modern Malaysia
eras.standfirst: After the trauma of 1969, the New Economic Policy reshaped society in favour of the Malay majority; Mahathir Mohamad drove rapid industrialisation; and after 2018 Malaysia entered an era of turbulent, competitive democracy.
body: The communal violence of 1969 prompted a far-reaching response. From 1971 the government adopted the New Economic Policy (NEP), a twenty-year programme of affirmative action intended to reduce poverty and to restructure society so that the Bumiputera, the Malays and other indigenous peoples, gained a much larger share of wealth, education and corporate ownership. The NEP transformed Malaysian society and politics, lifting many out of poverty while entrenching ethnic preferences that remain contested to this day. Through these decades the long-ruling Alliance, broadened into the Barisan Nasional coalition, dominated government.

From 1981 the country was led by Mahathir Mohamad, who served as prime minister for twenty-two years until 2003, the longest single tenure in Malaysian history. Mahathir drove an ambitious modernisation drive, promoting heavy industry, export manufacturing and landmark projects such as the Petronas Twin Towers, then briefly the tallest buildings in the world, and the Multimedia Super Corridor. His rule combined rapid growth with assertive nationalism and a centralisation of power, including the divisive dismissal and imprisonment of his deputy Anwar Ibrahim in 1998.

After six decades of unbroken Barisan Nasional rule, the corruption scandal surrounding the state fund 1MDB shattered the old order. In the 2018 general election an opposition alliance led by a returning Mahathir defeated the government of Najib Razak, who was later convicted and jailed over 1MDB. A period of instability followed, with the 2020 collapse of that government and rapid changes of prime minister, until Anwar Ibrahim, freed and rehabilitated after years in opposition, became prime minister in November 2022. Malaysia today is a multiethnic federal democracy navigating questions of corruption, ethnic policy and reform, its historic cities of Melaka and George Town recognised by UNESCO as a living monument to five centuries of trade and cultural exchange on the Straits of Malacca.

events.title: The New Economic Policy
events.summary: In the wake of the 1969 riots, the government launches a twenty-year programme of affirmative action to reduce poverty and raise the economic share of the Bumiputera majority, reshaping Malaysian society.

events.title: The Mahathir era and rapid modernisation
events.summary: Mahathir Mohamad serves as prime minister for twenty-two years, driving industrialisation and signature projects such as the Petronas Twin Towers while centralising political power.

events.title: Melaka and George Town inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: UNESCO lists Melaka and George Town, Historic Cities of the Straits of Malacca, recognising more than five centuries of trade and multicultural exchange between East and West.

events.title: 1MDB scandal ends Barisan Nasional rule
events.summary: Amid the 1MDB corruption scandal, an opposition alliance led by Mahathir defeats Najib Razak, ending more than six decades of Barisan Nasional government; Najib is later convicted and jailed.

events.title: Anwar Ibrahim becomes prime minister
events.summary: After years of imprisonment and opposition and a period of post-2020 instability, Anwar Ibrahim is appointed Malaysia's tenth prime minister following an inconclusive general election.

figures.name: Mahathir Mohamad
figures.blurb: Malaysia's longest-serving prime minister, who led a sweeping industrialisation drive and assertive nationalism over twenty-two years, then returned in 2018 at age 92 to head the alliance that toppled the scandal-hit Barisan Nasional government.

figures.name: Anwar Ibrahim
figures.blurb: Once Mahathir's deputy, dismissed and jailed in 1998, he spent two decades as a reform figurehead in opposition before becoming prime minister in November 2022, leading a multiethnic coalition pledged to clean government.

pullquote.text: Melaka and George Town have developed over 500 years of trading and cultural exchanges between East and West in the Straits of Malacca.

figures.name: Paramesvara
figures.blurb: Refugee prince of Palembang who founded Malacca around 1400; under him and his successors it became the leading Malay port and a centre from which Islam spread across the region.

figures.name: Tunku Abdul Rahman
figures.role: First Prime Minister of Malaya and Malaysia
figures.blurb: The 'Father of Independence' who proclaimed Merdeka in 1957, led the formation of Malaysia in 1963 and steered the early federation through Konfrontasi and Singapore's separation.

figures.name: Mahathir Mohamad
figures.blurb: Dominant figure of modern Malaysia who led an industrialisation drive across twenty-two years from 1981 and returned in 2018 to head the coalition that ended Barisan Nasional's long rule.
